title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper extends the framework of PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction sets to the few-shot meta-learning setting, where the learner has access to a sequence of i.i.d. tasks before encountering a new test task. In few-shot meta-learning, the primary goal is to adapt a predictor to a new task using only a few labeled examples. From an uncertainty quantification perspective, providing theoretically valid and non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is a challenging problem. Prior work by Fisch et al. [14] proposed meta-conformal prediction (Meta-CP), which leverages the task distribution to derive meaningful conformal prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees that hold over both the draw of calibration tasks and the test task. This paper adopts the same meta-learning setting but recasts it under the PAC prediction set framework, which offers high-probability guarantees about expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks and adaptation sets. In essence, the relationship between PAC sets and conformal prediction sets is mirrored in the relationship between Meta-PAC sets (proposed here) and Meta-CP sets (prior work). The paper empirically validates the effectiveness and theoretical properties of their algorithm on both simulated few-shot meta-learning benchmarks (including 5-shot 5-way image classification on mini-ImageNet, relation classification on FewRel, and robustness evaluation on CIFAR10-C) as well as a realistic medical prediction task (CDC Heart Dataset), where "tasks" are stratified by geographic region and time period.

## Strengths

### Meaningful Theoretical Contribution

The paper is well-written overall and makes a theoretically sound contribution that meaningfully extends prior work. The authors correctly identify and articulate an important gap in the Meta-CP framework: while Meta-CP provides marginal coverage guarantees that hold on average across all new tasks (marginalizing over the task draw), the proposed Meta-PAC framework provides guarantees that hold for a fixed calibration set when applied to many future tasks. This distinction is practically significant. As the authors note, in real-world deployment scenarios—such as autonomous vehicles deployed across multiple regions or healthcare systems serving different patient populations—one typically fixes a calibration set during the meta-learning phase and then applies the same model to many subsequent tasks. The Meta-PAC guarantee is more appropriate for this setting, as it ensures correctness with high probability over future tasks, given the fixed calibration data. This is a meaningful and useful extension to the existing Meta-CP procedure, and the distinction is clearly articulated through the graphical models presented in Figure 2.

### Sound Calibration Strategy

The calibration strategy employed in Algorithm 1 is elegant and correct. Upon initial inspection, one might worry that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the N calibration tasks (Line 3 of Algorithm 1) would require a union bound correction factor of 1/N to ensure validity simultaneously across all calibration tasks. However, the authors cleverly avoid this inefficiency. The key insight is that the PAC property itself only requires that the *output* of the PAC method (i.e., the estimated threshold $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$) be i.i.d. random variables with certain properties, not that each individual threshold necessarily satisfies its corresponding $\epsilon$-correctness condition. Since the thresholds are i.i.d. draws from the same underlying distribution, one can directly apply a PAC prediction set algorithm to these threshold values (Line 5) using only two factors of 1/2 (from the two applications of PAC prediction sets) rather than requiring a union bound over all N tasks. This is a more efficient construction and demonstrates careful technical design. The authors acknowledge this subtlety in their response to concerns about "imposter" thresholds, and this understanding reflects a solid grasp of the underlying probability theory.

### Strong Empirical Validation

The experimental results are convincing and demonstrate the practical utility of the proposed approach across diverse domains. On mini-ImageNet (Figure 3), the proposed Meta-PS achieves the target error rate of $\epsilon = 0.1$ with at least $1 - \alpha = 0.9$ probability, while competing baselines either fail to satisfy this guarantee empirically (PS, Meta-CP) or are overly conservative (PS-Test). Importantly, Figure 3c provides compelling evidence that Meta-PS controls error with respect to the randomness in the test adaptation set—a key distinction from Meta-CP. The results on FewRel (Figure 4) exhibit similar trends, with Meta-PS producing smaller prediction sets than PS-Test while maintaining the desired coverage guarantee. The inclusion of the CDC Heart Dataset experiments is particularly valuable, as it demonstrates applicability to real-world medical prediction tasks where uncertainty quantification is critical. The dataset spans 10 years of CDC survey data across multiple US states, providing a realistic testbed where tasks are naturally defined by geographic and temporal stratification. The consistent empirical validation of the theoretical guarantees across these diverse domains (computer vision, natural language processing, and healthcare) strengthens confidence in the approach's generality and practical relevance.

### Clear Problem Motivation

The paper provides excellent motivation for why the meta-learning setting requires specialized treatment. The autonomous driving example in the introduction and Figure 1 effectively illustrates why guarantees that hold conditionally on a fixed calibration set (and adapted to each new task) are more appropriate than marginal guarantees. The example of deploying an image classifier to a new region (Alaska) with minimal additional labeling clearly demonstrates a realistic use case where the proposed Meta-PAC guarantee is preferable to alternatives.

## Weaknesses

### Presentation and Notation Burden

While the paper is generally well-written, the presentation of the main algorithmic and theoretical contributions in Section 4 is dense and notation-heavy. The reader is immediately confronted with formal definitions and mathematical notation without sufficient high-level intuition. A more accessible introduction to Section 4 would significantly improve readability. For instance, before diving into Definition 3 and the formal problem setup, the paper would benefit from a plain-English explanation of the core idea. At a high level, the Meta-PAC framework aims to find a single, shared threshold $\tau$ that:

1. On average (with high probability $1 - \delta$) over the random draw of calibration tasks used to choose this threshold,
2. and on average (with high probability $1 - \alpha$) over the random choice of the test task and its adaptation set,
3. and on average (with high probability $1 - \epsilon$) over predictions the model makes on evaluation examples from the new task,

the prediction set $F_\tau^A(x)$ contains the correct label $y$.

This intuitive framing appears implicitly throughout the manuscript (particularly in Figure 1's caption and the discussion following Definition 3), but stating it plainly and directly at the beginning of Section 4 would greatly enhance accessibility without sacrificing rigor. The current presentation requires readers to extract this intuition from scattered discussions rather than having it presented as a clear roadmap.

### Fixed Threshold Limitation

A notable limitation of the proposed approach is that the threshold $\tau$ is fixed across all downstream tasks and never updated, regardless of the adaptation set size or apparent task difficulty. This design choice contrasts with Meta-CP (as implemented in [14]), which attempts to estimate a data-dependent, task-conditional threshold through quantile prediction. While the fixed threshold approach simplifies the algorithm and preserves the PAC guarantee (as discussed in Section 4.2), it potentially sacrifices adaptivity and test-task conditional tightness. In scenarios where there is significant variability across tasks or where the adaptation set is sufficiently large to reliably estimate task-specific statistics, a task-conditional threshold could yield substantially smaller prediction sets. For example, if the new task is significantly easier or harder than the calibration tasks, a fixed threshold may be overly conservative or insufficiently conservative, respectively. The paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of this trade-off and perhaps empirical comparison showing how much efficiency is lost by fixing the threshold. This limitation becomes particularly relevant in settings where adaptation sets grow beyond the few-shot regime.

### Practical Scalability Concerns

The requirement for several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks, while theoretically justified, raises practical concerns about applicability. In the experiments, the authors use N = 500 calibration tasks for mini-ImageNet and FewRel, and N = 250 for the CDC Heart Dataset. While these numbers are achievable in the experimental settings studied (where tasks can be sampled from a large pool of base classes or geographic regions), many real-world meta-learning scenarios may not have access to such abundant task diversity. The reliance on Clopper-Pearson bounds (Algorithm 2 in Appendix B) is theoretically sound but can be quite conservative when calibration task sizes are small. The paper acknowledges this limitation in Section 6, noting that "the current approach requires enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee," but this deserves more prominent discussion in the main text. A

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning, addressing the important problem of uncertainty quantification in few-shot learning scenarios. The core contribution is a method to construct prediction sets that can be adapted to new tasks with only a few labeled examples while maintaining theoretical guarantees. Specifically, the authors introduce a meta-PAC (mPAC) guarantee that holds with high probability over future tasks, which is a meaningful extension of standard PAC guarantees to the meta-learning setting. Experiments on four datasets spanning three distinct application domains—mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C for vision, FewRel for natural language processing, and the CDC Heart Dataset for healthcare—demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed approach. The method produces prediction sets that satisfy the PAC guarantee while maintaining smaller set sizes compared to baseline approaches that also satisfy these guarantees.

## Strengths

* **Well-structured presentation and clarity**: The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear motivation and exposition. The use of graphical models in Figure 2 to distinguish the proposed meta-PAC guarantee from existing approaches (marginal coverage, standard PAC, and meta task-marginal guarantees) is particularly effective. This visual comparison helps readers quickly understand the key innovation: the proposed method controls error with respect to three sources of randomness (calibration tasks, adaptation sets, and test data), providing fine-grained control that existing methods lack. The progression from background material through the problem formulation to the proposed solution is logical and easy to follow.

* **Theoretically sound approach with meaningful extension**: The proposed method is not merely a straightforward application of existing PAC prediction set techniques to meta-learning. Instead, the authors carefully design a two-step algorithm that addresses the unique challenges of meta-learning. The meta-PAC definition (Definition 3) is a principled extension of standard PAC guarantees that captures the hierarchical nature of meta-learning with its multiple sources of randomness. Theorem 1 provides a formal guarantee that the proposed Algorithm 1 satisfies this definition. The theoretical contribution is substantial because it identifies and formalizes an important gap in existing work—specifically, that prior meta-conformal prediction methods (e.g., [14]) do not control error conditional on the adaptation set, which is crucial for practical deployment.

* **Comprehensive experimental evaluation across diverse domains**: The experimental validation spans multiple application domains and datasets, which strengthens the claims about the method's general applicability. The inclusion of the CDC Heart Dataset is particularly noteworthy, as it demonstrates relevance to safety-critical healthcare applications. The experiments include appropriate baselines (PS, PS-Test, Meta-CP) that represent different design choices, and the results consistently show that the proposed Meta-PS approach satisfies the desired error guarantee while producing smaller prediction sets than conservative baselines. The use of box plots to visualize the distribution of errors and set sizes across multiple random trials provides a transparent view of the method's performance variability.

## Weaknesses

* **Fixed prediction set parameters after adaptation raises design questions**: A key design choice in the proposed method is the reuse of the same meta prediction set parameter τ̂(S, A) after adaptation to the test task, without updating this parameter using the test task's adaptation set. While the authors argue that this is valid because "the meta prediction set parameter is also chosen after adaptation via a hold-out adaptation set from the same distribution as each respective calibration set" (Section 4.2), this argument could be strengthened with more rigorous justification. Specifically, the paper would benefit from deeper analysis of whether adapting the prediction set parameter itself (in addition to the score function) could lead to tighter prediction sets without violating the mPAC guarantee. The current approach appears to prioritize simplicity and label efficiency (avoiding additional calibration on the test task) over potential improvements in set size. A theoretical or empirical investigation of this trade-off would be valuable. Additionally, it is unclear how sensitive the method is to the quality of the adaptation set used during meta-calibration—if the adaptation sets Si used to learn τ̂i are not representative of the test adaptation set A, the reuse assumption may be violated in practice.

* **Limited scope of meta-learning algorithms evaluated**: The experimental evaluation primarily uses prototypical networks (a metric-based meta-learning approach) as the score function. While the authors claim in Section 3.2 that the method can work with "any meta learning method, including metric-based meta-learning [11] and optimization-based meta-learning [12]," the experiments do not validate this claim empirically. Optimization-based meta-learning methods such as Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) [12] represent an important class of approaches that differ fundamentally in how they adapt the predictor to new tasks. MAML performs gradient-based optimization on the test task, whereas prototypical networks compute task-adapted representations without gradient updates. It would be valuable to demonstrate that the proposed PAC prediction set method maintains its theoretical guarantees and practical performance when used with MAML or other optimization-based approaches. The lack of such experiments limits the generality of the claims and leaves open questions about whether the method's performance depends on the specific characteristics of metric-based adaptation. Additionally, the paper does not discuss potential challenges in applying the method to more complex meta-learning scenarios, such as those involving domain adaptation or cross-domain few-shot learning.

* **Limited experimental complexity in few-shot classification settings**: The mini-ImageNet experiments focus exclusively on 5-way classification, which represents a relatively modest classification difficulty. The few-shot learning community has moved toward evaluating on more challenging settings such as 10-way or 20-way classification, which better stress-test the robustness of uncertainty quantification methods. Evaluating on more challenging classification scenarios would strengthen claims about the method's practical utility and would help identify potential limitations of the approach. For instance, as the number of classes increases, the prediction sets may grow larger or the PAC guarantees may require larger adaptation sets to maintain the same error rates. The paper would be strengthened by exploring these scaling properties. Additionally, while CIFAR10-C results are mentioned as being in Appendix E, the main text does not discuss these results or compare them with mini-ImageNet, making it difficult to assess performance across different visual domains and corruption types.

## Questions and Suggestions for Clarification

* **Regarding the reuse of prediction set parameters**: Could the authors provide theoretical or empirical analysis of what happens if the adaptation set A used during test time differs significantly in distribution from the adaptation sets Ai used during meta-calibration? How robust is the mPAC guarantee to such distribution shift?

* **Regarding generalization to other meta-learning algorithms**: Can the authors provide at least one experiment (even in the appendix) demonstrating that the method works with MAML or another optimization-based meta-learning algorithm? This would substantiate the claim that the method is algorithm-agnostic.

* **Regarding classification difficulty**: What is the computational and sample complexity of the method as the number of classes increases? Would the method still be practical for 20-way or higher classification?

* **Regarding the choice of hyperparameters**: How were the hyperparameters (ε, α, δ) chosen in the experiments? Is there guidance for practitioners on setting these values for new applications?

## Technical Soundness

The paper appears technically sound. The theoretical analysis is rigorous, with Theorem 1 correctly stating that Algorithm 1 produces an (ε, α, δ)-mPAC estimator. The proof strategy of constructing a prediction interval over scalar parameters that accounts for the hierarchical randomness in meta-learning is conceptually clear. The experimental methodology is appropriate, using multiple random trials to evaluate the empirical satisfaction of the mPAC guarantee. The authors properly distinguish their contribution from prior work (particularly [14]) by showing that their guarantee is conditional on the adaptation set, whereas prior work's guarantee is not.

## Minor Issues and Suggestions

* The paper could benefit from more discussion of the sample complexity requirements. While the authors mention in Section 6 that "the current approach requires enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee" and reference [3] for analysis, more explicit guidance on the number of calibration tasks N and calibration examples per task n would be helpful for practitioners.

* The connection between the scalar parameterization of prediction sets and the expectation that set size decreases with increasing τ could be explained more intuitively in the main text before diving into the algorithm.

* It would be helpful to include a discussion of computational complexity—specifically, how does the computational cost scale with the number of calibration tasks N and the size of adaptation sets?

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The authors appropriately acknowledge limitations in Section 6, noting that the method requires sufficient calibration data and discussing potential misuse of the guarantee without proper understanding of assumptions. The discussion of societal impact in Appendix F is appreciated, though it could be more prominent in the main text given the application to safety-critical domains like healthcare.

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by extending PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning setting with a principled three-parameter guarantee that controls multiple sources of randomness. The proposed algorithm is practical and the experimental validation across multiple

reviews.summary: This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

This paper addresses an important and timely problem: constructing prediction sets with probably approximately correct (PAC) guarantees in the meta-learning setting, where a model must quickly adapt to new tasks with limited labeled data. The authors recognize that existing PAC prediction set frameworks, while well-developed for standard machine learning scenarios, do not adequately capture the unique characteristics and requirements of meta-learning. The paper proposes a meta-PAC (mPAC) notion that accounts for multiple sources of randomness inherent in meta-learning: randomness from calibration task distributions, randomness from adaptation sets for new tasks, and randomness from test data itself.

The core contribution is the introduction of a three-parameter PAC guarantee (Definition 3) that controls error with respect to three distinct sources of randomness. Unlike existing meta conformal prediction approaches [14], which provide guarantees only on average across future tasks, the proposed framework provides guarantees that hold with high probability for each individual future task, conditioned on its adaptation set. This distinction is particularly important for safety-critical applications such as autonomous vehicles and healthcare, where correctness guarantees must hold for specific deployment contexts rather than in aggregate.

The authors propose Algorithm 1 (META-PS), a two-step procedure that first constructs PAC prediction sets for each calibration task distribution, then uses these learned parameters as a new dataset to construct a PAC prediction set over the space of parameters itself. This elegant approach leverages the i.i.d. nature of calibration task distributions to ensure that the selected parameter will be appropriate for new test distributions drawn from the same meta-distribution p_π.

## Strengths

**1. Novel and Well-Motivated Problem Formulation**

The paper makes a compelling case for why existing PAC prediction set frameworks are insufficient for meta-learning. The distinction between the meta task-marginal guarantee (Figure 2c) and the proposed meta PAC guarantee (Figure 2d) is clearly articulated and well-illustrated. The graphical model representation in Figure 2 effectively communicates the key difference: the proposed approach conditions the correctness guarantee on the adaptation set A from a test task, ensuring that the guarantee holds for future examples from that specific adapted model. This is a meaningful advance over prior work that only guarantees correctness on average across tasks.

The motivating example of learning image classifiers for self-driving cars across different regions (New York, Pennsylvania, California, Alaska) effectively demonstrates why task-specific guarantees are necessary in practice. The authors correctly identify that in safety-critical domains, one needs to know that the prediction set will perform reliably in each deployment region, not just on average across regions.

**2. Technically Sound Theoretical Framework**

The paper is mathematically rigorous and the theoretical analysis is clear and well-structured. Definition 3 formally captures the meta-PAC notion with appropriate conditioning on different sources of randomness. Theorem 1 provides a guarantee that Algorithm 1 produces an (ε, α, δ)-mPAC estimator. The proof strategy, which leverages the i.i.d. nature of calibration task distributions and constructs a prediction interval over parameters, is intuitive and well-motivated.

The connection between the proposed approach and existing PAC prediction set theory [3, 21] is clearly established. The authors effectively build on the scalar parameterization of prediction sets to minimize expected prediction set size while satisfying the PAC constraint, a principle inherited from prior work but appropriately adapted to the meta-learning setting.

The assumption that the meta-learned score function f is given as input to the algorithm is reasonable and allows the framework to be agnostic to the specific meta-learning algorithm employed (whether metric-based like prototypical networks or optimization-based like MAML). This modularity is a practical strength.

**3. Comprehensive Experimental Validation Across Multiple Domains**

The experimental evaluation is thorough and spans three distinct application domains: computer vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), natural language processing (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset). This breadth demonstrates the generality of the approach. The experiments are designed to empirically validate the theoretical guarantees, which is appropriate and informative.

The comparison against multiple baselines is instructive. The naive PAC prediction set approach (PS) that pools all calibration data fails to respect the meta-learning structure and does not empirically satisfy the mPAC criterion. The PS-Test baseline, which uses 20 shots from the test distribution, is overly conservative as it essentially treats the test distribution as known. The Meta-CP baseline, representing prior work on meta conformal prediction, fails to control error conditional on the adaptation set, exactly as predicted by the theoretical analysis. The proposed Meta-PS approach consistently satisfies the desired ε error bound with probability at least 1-α over test adaptation sets and 1-δ over calibration data.

Figure 3c and Figures 6a-b provide particularly compelling evidence that the proposed approach successfully controls the prediction set error with respect to randomness in the test adaptation set, whereas Meta-CP does not. This empirical validation of the key theoretical distinction is valuable.

The prediction set sizes produced by Meta-PS are smaller than the conservative PS-Test baseline while still satisfying the ε error constraint, suggesting that the approach achieves a good trade-off between coverage and efficiency.

**4. Clear Presentation and Accessibility**

The paper is well-written and easy to follow. The progression from background (Section 3) to the proposed framework (Section 4) is logical. The use of graphical models in Figure 2 to illustrate different guarantee types is an excellent pedagogical choice. The algorithm is presented in clear pseudocode (Algorithm 1) that is easy to understand and implement.

The distinction between meta-learning with and without adaptation is clearly explained, and the paper appropriately handles both cases. The appendix provides additional details on the simpler case without adaptation (Appendix A), which helps clarify the main contribution.

## Weaknesses and Questions

**1. Computational Complexity of Grid Search in Algorithm 2**

Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM), which is used internally by Algorithm 1, involves a grid search over possible threshold values τ. While the authors mention that "any PAC prediction set algorithms are used to implement an estimator ˆε,δ," the specific implementation via grid search raises important practical questions that are not adequately addressed in the paper.

Specifically, the computational cost of the grid search depends critically on several factors: (1) the granularity of the grid—how finely τ is discretized; (2) the range of τ values searched; and (3) the cost of evaluating the PAC constraint for each candidate τ value. The paper provides no discussion of how these parameters are set in practice, whether any adaptive refinement strategies are employed, or how sensitive the results are to the granularity choice.

For the experiments described, what grid granularity was used? Does the computational cost scale problematically as the number of calibration tasks N increases? Could the authors provide explicit timing comparisons between their approach and the baselines, particularly Meta-CP? 

While the paper claims that "we can use any PAC prediction set algorithms," it would be valuable to discuss whether there are more efficient alternatives to grid search, such as binary search (if the feasible region is convex), or whether the authors have explored such alternatives. For practical deployment, especially in settings where the approach must adapt to new tasks frequently, computational efficiency could be a significant concern.

**2. Limited Discussion of Sample Complexity Requirements**

The authors acknowledge in Section 6 that "a limitation of the current approach is that it requires enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee" and reference [3] for analysis. However, this limitation deserves more thorough discussion within the main paper.

Specifically, how many calibration tasks N and calibration examples per task n are actually required to achieve reasonable error bounds? The experiments use N=500 for mini-ImageNet and FewRel, and N=250 for CDC Heart Dataset. Are these numbers necessary, or are they conservative choices? 

The paper would benefit from explicit sample complexity bounds or empirical analysis showing how the mPAC guarantee degrades as N and n decrease. This is particularly important because meta-learning is often motivated by scenarios with limited labeled data. If the approach requires large numbers of calibration tasks and examples to satisfy the guarantee, this could limit its applicability in some practical scenarios.

Furthermore, the paper does not discuss how to determine appropriate values for the hyperparameters ε, α, and δ in practice. What guidance can be provided to practitioners about setting these parameters? How sensitive are the results to these choices?

**3. Limited Exploration of Failure Modes and Robustness**

While the experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach works well on four datasets, there is limited discussion of scenarios where the approach might fail or perform poorly. For instance:

- What happens if the test task distribution p_θ is significantly different from the calibration task distributions? The i.i.d. assumption on task distributions is crucial to the theoretical guarantee, but the paper does not empirically explore robustness to violations of this assumption.

- How does the approach perform when the meta
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct. The theoretical development appears sound throughout, with careful attention to the mathematical formulations and their implications for the meta-learning setting.

I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before. The specific contribution of nesting the guarantee twice—once over calibration sets and once over adaptation sets—represents a novel combination of concentration arguments that, while conceptually incremental, does provide a more granular control over different sources of randomness in the meta-learning pipeline.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal prediction for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. [14] in that it also conditions on the tasks and adaptation sets separately. The distinction illustrated in Figure 2d versus Figure 2c is meaningful: the proposed meta-PAC guarantee provides separate control over randomness from calibration tasks, test task adaptation sets, and test examples, whereas prior work only controls these sources jointly. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

---

## Overview

This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost term is an expectation on a 0-1 loss (Definition 1), representing the empirical error rate on test examples. This expectation is nested within two outer probabilities: an inner probability conditioned on the calibration set and meta-learned parameters (the (ε, α)-guarantee over test tasks and adaptation sets), and an outer probability accounting for randomness in the calibration phase itself (the δ parameter). The algorithm, presented as Algorithm 1, boils down to two sequential applications of binomial concentration bounds (specifically using the PS-BINOM algorithm from Appendix B). The paper then provides extensive experiments on several meta-learning tasks spanning vision (mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and medicine (CDC Heart Dataset).

---

## Strengths

**Technical Correctness**: The technical results are clearly correct. The proof structure in Appendix C properly establishes that the two-stage concentration argument yields the claimed meta-PAC guarantee. The authors carefully track which random variables are conditioned on at each stage, and the mathematical development properly accounts for the three sources of randomness identified in Section 4.

**Comprehensive Experimental Evaluation**: The experiments are comprehensive and well-designed. The paper evaluates the approach across four datasets spanning three distinct application domains, which demonstrates breadth. The experimental setup is detailed (Section 5 and Appendix D), including specific numbers for N (calibration tasks), n (calibration examples per task), and t (adaptation examples). The choice to conduct 100 random trials for calibration and 50 random trials per calibration set for test evaluation provides reasonable statistical coverage of the variance in the guarantees. The use of box plots in Figures 3, 4, and 5 effectively communicates the empirical distribution of errors and set sizes.

**Clear Problem Motivation**: The motivating example of autonomous driving across regions (Figure 1) effectively illustrates why task-conditional guarantees matter in practice. The distinction between guarantees that hold "on average across tasks" versus "for each task" is practically important and well-articulated. The paper clearly explains why existing approaches like meta conformal prediction [14] may be insufficient when one needs guarantees that hold for a specific new region or cohort.

**Algorithmic Clarity**: Algorithm 1 is relatively simple to implement, requiring only two applications of existing PAC prediction set algorithms (such as PS-BINOM). This modularity is a strength—practitioners can plug in different base PAC prediction set algorithms depending on their specific needs. The reuse of the same threshold parameter τ after adaptation (without requiring additional calibration on the test distribution) is practically efficient.

---

## Weaknesses

### (1) Interpretability and Practical Usability of the Guarantee

The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used in practice. The three-parameter specification (ε, α, δ) introduces substantial cognitive overhead compared to standard confidence intervals or even single-parameter conformal prediction guarantees.

Consider the practical challenge: a practitioner must now simultaneously choose three parameters with distinct interpretations:
- ε controls the allowable error rate on test examples
- α controls the probability of correctness over test task adaptation sets
- δ controls the probability of correctness over calibration tasks

In the experiments, the authors set δ = 10^{-5} for Meta-PS while using δ = 10^{-5} for baselines, but it is unclear how a practitioner should calibrate these choices for their own application. The paper provides no principled guidance on how to set these parameters as a function of domain-specific risk tolerances or cost-benefit analyses. For instance, in a medical application (CDC Heart Dataset), should one prioritize ε (individual prediction accuracy) or α (consistency across different patient cohorts)? The paper does not address this fundamental question.

Furthermore, the guarantee itself becomes difficult to communicate to stakeholders. In safety-critical applications like healthcare or autonomous vehicles, one must often explain the uncertainty quantification approach to non-technical decision-makers. A statement like "with probability at least 1-δ over calibration tasks, the prediction set will contain the true label with probability at least 1-α over test tasks, with error at most ε" is orders of magnitude more complex than "our model is 95% accurate" and requires substantial statistical literacy to understand properly.

The tolerance interval interpretation, which is already cognitively demanding for many practitioners, becomes even more challenging when nested within two additional probability statements. The authors acknowledge this limitation implicitly by noting in Appendix F that "a user of the proposed algorithm might misuse the guarantee without a proper understanding of assumptions," but this acknowledgment does not resolve the fundamental interpretability problem.

### (2) Presentation and Readability

The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. While the authors made revisions post-discussion, several presentation issues remain that limit accessibility.

**Figure 2 Analysis**: Figure 2 attempts to compare different guarantee structures using graphical models, but the presentation is confusing. The figure includes extraneous information and the color scheme (mentioned in the caption) does not adequately distinguish between different stripe patterns in the printed version. Critical aspects remain unexplained: What exactly do the orientations and densities of the stripes represent? Why is the orientation different between panels? The box labeled "N" with a plate notation is not explained in the main text—readers unfamiliar with plate notation in graphical models will struggle. The figure is central to understanding the paper's main contribution (the distinction between the proposed meta-PAC guarantee and prior work), yet its presentation obscures rather than clarifies this distinction. A more detailed figure caption or an additional explanatory paragraph would significantly improve comprehension.

**Section 4.1 Complexity**: Section 4.1 is dense and difficult to parse. The setup introduces multiple indexed families of distributions (p_θ₀ᵢ, p_θᵢ, p_θ) and multiple indexed sample sets (S, A, evaluation sets) without sufficient intuitive scaffolding. The distinction between calibration distributions, test distributions, and the task distribution p_π is not immediately clear. A concrete running example (e.g., the autonomous driving scenario from Figure 1) worked through in detail in Section 4.1 would significantly aid comprehension. The current presentation jumps quickly to formal definitions without building intuition first.

**Notation Overload**: The paper uses heavy notation (S ∈ Z^{nN}, A ∈ Z^{tN}, etc.) that becomes difficult to track across sections. While necessary for formal development, the paper would benefit from a notation table or more frequent reminders of what each symbol represents. The use of both primed and unprimed variables (e.g., f vs. f^A vs. f_{A_i}) adds cognitive load.

**Accessibility to Broader Audience**: The authors correctly note that "a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees" will fully understand the work. However, this observation suggests that the paper's impact may be limited to a narrow audience. While this is not inherently a weakness for a specialized venue like NeurIPS, it does suggest that the paper would benefit from additional exposition to bridge the gap between the specialized PAC prediction set community and the broader machine learning community interested in uncertainty quantification and meta-learning.

### (3) Limited Technical Novelty

The technical result, while correct, is not particularly novel. The meta conformal prediction paper by Fisch et al. [14] already provides a guarantee in this problem
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

## Summary

The paper proposes a method for constructing prediction sets in the meta-testing stage by leveraging prediction sets estimated during the meta-training adaptation and calibration phases. The approach is carefully designed to satisfy PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) guarantees, providing theoretical assurances for generating meta-test prediction sets without requiring additional calibration on the test task. The proposed Meta-PS algorithm is evaluated across four datasets spanning three distinct application domains—computer vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), natural language processing (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset)—demonstrating competitive empirical results that align with the theoretical guarantees.

## Strengths

1. **Strong Theoretical Contribution**: The paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by extending PAC prediction set guarantees to the meta-learning setting. The proposed (ε, α, δ)-meta PAC (mPAC) definition in Definition 3 is well-motivated and represents a meaningful extension beyond existing work. The distinction from prior meta-conformal prediction approaches (as illustrated in Figure 2) is particularly valuable, as it provides finer-grained control over correctness guarantees by conditioning on both the adaptation set and test data, rather than only on the entire test task. This is an important distinction for practical applications where task-specific guarantees are necessary.

2. **Rigorous Theoretical Guarantee**: The proposed method is backed by a formal PAC bound (Theorem 1), which provides confidence that the prediction sets will satisfy the specified error constraints with high probability. The proof structure, which constructs an interval over scalar parameters of prediction sets to account for the hierarchical nature of meta-learning, is sound and well-reasoned. This theoretical grounding is particularly important for safety-critical applications mentioned in the introduction, such as healthcare and autonomous vehicles.

3. **Practical Algorithm Design**: Algorithm 1 is elegantly designed with a two-step procedure that is both intuitive and implementable. The meta-calibration step first constructs prediction set parameters for each calibration task, and the second step constructs a prediction interval over these parameters. Importantly, the algorithm allows for the use of any existing PAC prediction set algorithm (such as PS-BINOM) as a subroutine, making it modular and flexible. This modularity is a significant practical advantage, as it allows practitioners to plug in improved prediction set construction methods as they become available.

4. **Comprehensive Experimental Validation**: The empirical evaluation spans multiple domains and demonstrates that the proposed approach empirically satisfies the mPAC criterion while producing smaller prediction sets compared to baselines that also satisfy the error constraint. The experimental design with 100 random trials for calibration and 50 trials for test datasets per calibration set provides reasonable statistical confidence in the results. The inclusion of diverse datasets (vision, NLP, and healthcare) strengthens the claim of generalizability.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Presentation and Clarity Issues**: The paper suffers from significant presentation problems that impede comprehension and accessibility. The notation is dense and sometimes confusing, particularly regarding the score functions and their different parameterizations. For instance, the distinction between f, f^A, and F_τ could be explained more clearly upfront. The paper assumes substantial familiarity with prior work on PAC prediction sets [3], and readers unfamiliar with this foundational material will struggle to follow the main contributions. The authors should consider dedicating more space to intuitive explanations before diving into formal definitions. For example, the intuition behind why we need three sources of randomness (δ, α, ε) could be explained more gradually rather than introduced abruptly in Section 4.1.

2. **Missing Big Picture in Figure 1**: While Figure 1 is intended to illustrate the overall framework, it lacks crucial context that would help readers, particularly those from the meta-learning community, understand the setup. Specifically, the figure does not clearly indicate that f is a pre-trained meta-learned model that serves as the foundation for the entire approach. The distinction between the meta-learning phase (where f is learned), the calibration phase (where the prediction set parameter τ is chosen), and the adaptation/inference phase (where the same τ is reused) could be made more explicit. Adding a brief caption explaining this three-phase structure would significantly improve clarity. Additionally, the role of the calibration set in choosing τ independently of the test task could be highlighted more prominently.

3. **Incomplete Justification for Key Design Choices**: The paper does not adequately explain why the score functions in different parts of the algorithm have different input spaces. Specifically, in the meta-calibration step (line 3 of Algorithm 1), the score function f^{A_i} is adapted using the adaptation set A_i from the calibration task. However, the distinction between how f is used in the meta-training phase versus how it is adapted in the calibration and test phases could be explained more carefully. The paper states that "f is a pre-trained meta-learned model," but the exact mechanism by which this model is adapted (whether through gradient-based updates, metric learning, or other methods) is not discussed in the main paper. While Appendix A provides some details, this information should be more prominently featured in the main text.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Limited Evaluation of Estimator Choice**: The authors select PS-BINOM (Algorithm 2 in Appendix B) to implement the PAC prediction set estimator ψ_{ε,δ}, but do not thoroughly investigate whether different estimators would yield significantly different performance characteristics. The paper briefly mentions that "any PAC prediction set algorithms are used to implement an estimator ψ_{ε,δ}," but provides no ablation study or discussion of how the choice of estimator affects the final prediction set size or error. This is a notable gap, as the quality of the prediction sets depends critically on this choice. An ablation study comparing different PAC prediction set algorithms would strengthen the empirical contribution and provide practitioners with guidance on which estimators to use in different settings.

2. **Limited Diversity in Meta-Learned Models**: The experimental evaluation relies exclusively on prototypical networks as the score function across all datasets. While prototypical networks are a reasonable choice for few-shot learning, the paper would benefit from evaluating the proposed approach with other meta-learning methods, such as MAML (Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning) [12] or other optimization-based approaches mentioned in the related work. This would demonstrate the generality of the approach and provide insights into how different meta-learning strategies interact with the PAC prediction set construction. The claim that the method is agnostic to the choice of meta-learning algorithm would be strengthened by empirical validation across multiple approaches.

3. **Unclear Trade-off Between Error and Set Size**: In the experimental results (Figures 3, 4, and 5), both prediction set error and prediction set size are reported as evaluation metrics. However, the paper does not provide clear guidance on how to interpret the trade-off between these two objectives. In some cases, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but larger set size compared to other methods, while in other cases the opposite is true. A more explicit analysis of the Pareto frontier or a composite metric that balances error and size would help readers understand when Meta-PS is preferable. For instance, are there settings where slightly violating the error constraint is acceptable in exchange for significantly smaller prediction sets? The paper should clarify the practical implications of these trade-offs.

4. **Insufficient Background Material**: The paper is not entirely self-contained, and readers unfamiliar with the prior work on PAC prediction sets [3, 21] will encounter significant comprehension difficulties. While Section 3.1 provides a brief overview of PAC prediction sets, the exposition is quite terse and assumes familiarity with concepts like "training-conditional" guarantees and "tolerance regions." The paper would benefit from a more detailed explanation of why PAC prediction sets are necessary (as opposed to standard marginal coverage guarantees from conformal prediction), and how the training-conditional aspect differs from marginal coverage in practical terms. A worked example showing how the algorithm operates on a simple synthetic dataset would also help readers understand the method.

5. **Notation Consistency and Clarity**: The notation throughout the paper is quite heavy and sometimes inconsistent. For example, the use of subscripts and superscripts for different components of the score function (f, f^A, f^{A_i}) is potentially confusing. Additionally, the distinction between calibration sets S and adaptation sets A could be made more explicit earlier in the paper. The notation S = (S_1, ..., S_N) for the collection of calibration sets is introduced without sufficient motivation for why this hierarchical structure is necessary. While these notational choices are ultimately reasonable, introducing them more gradually with clear definitions would improve readability.

## Specific Technical Questions and Concerns

1. **Comparison Between Meta-CP and Meta-PS**: While Figure 3c and Figure 6 provide evidence that Meta-PS controls error over test adaptation sets (satisfying the 1-α guarantee), the overall comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS is not entirely clear. Both

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
