reviews.summary:
In this paper, the authors:
- provide theory for memory capacity (at a given classification margin) for perceptrons with distribution-constrained weights, and that capacity loss from optimum can be phrased as the Wasserstein distance between the target distribution and standard normal
- design a novel learning algorithm that account for both classification loss and distribution matching (via optimal transport)
- apply to a perceptron with balanced E & I input populations
- investigate the optimal prior for a student neuron in a task where the student neuron is asked to match the teacher's weight distribution given only the teacher's input-output response
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
originality: in my limited knowledge, I do not know of other related works that investigate distribution-constrained weights of a network from a theoretical / statistical mechanics point of view

quality: the proof itself (section 2.2) is beyond me, but at a superficial level, the main result (Fig 2-4) seem solid and that simulations support the theoretical prediction. I don't really get how the teacher-student learning paradigm is relevant, however. It seems to be a bit of a contrived setting: even though it makes sense from a decoding perspective (as a neuroscientist trying to infer the weights of a neuron given only its input and outputs), no biological neuron would be required to do this as the student.

clarity: the work is clearly presented, with high quality and thoughtful visualizations, as well as an easy-to-follow high level narrative. 

significance: the high level motivation of distribution-matched learning is clearly stated, and of significant interest to the neuroscience community at large. However, the amount of assumptions / simplifications required to arrive at the actual results dampens my enthusiasm (expanded below)
reviews.questions:
As stated above, the question of how task optimization and weight distribution matching can be simultaneously achieved, given experimental observations of log-normally distributed weights, is a very important question. However, the paper starts with this motivation, but essentially solves the problem for a single perceptron (or, uni-dimensional output logistic regression) with random inputs. I understand that assumptions are necessary for such theoretical work, which I believe is of high quality. But the massive reduction in scope feels almost like a bait and switch (though I'm sure the authors do not intend for this to be the case). At the very least, some investigation on a system that relaxes one or several of the assumptions should be done, even if the theory does not end up so clean:

- would the theory / simulation results hold for non-random inputs? Basically in no scenario does the real brain have to perform classification on perceptually random inputs, so at the minimum, a toy experiment on structured inputs would be justified (MNIST 0 vs 1, for example).

- similarly, the paper is motivated by neural "networks", so how do the results hold up for more than a single neuron? For higher dimensional outputs? Do compensation of weights for different neurons violate the theory?

- I don't see how the results of Figure 5 is relevant to the overall story. Perhaps more justification or clarity in writing needs to be provided

- in the learning algorithm, why does distribution matching have to occur separately from weight optimization? I suppose it fits nicely with the optimal transport theory, but simply optimizing a loss function that combines task performance and, e.g., DKL or Wasserstein distance between the weight distributions, simultaneously would be naively reasonable, and no less biologically implausible (since individual weights do not know of the other weights and certainly does not actively converge to some target lognormal distribution).
reviews.limitations: Some discussion of future works, but very little discussion of limitations in section 6. No discussion of societal impact (but none necessary)
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors:
- provide theory for memory capacity (at a given classification margin) for perceptrons with distribution-constrained weights, and that capacity loss from optimum can be phrased as the Wasserstein distance between the target distribution and standard normal
- design a novel learning algorithm that account for both classification loss and distribution matching (via optimal transport)
- apply to a perceptron with balanced E & I input populations
- investigate the optimal prior for a student neuron in a task where the student neuron is asked to match the teacher's weight distribution given only the teacher's input-output response

Strengths And Weaknesses:
originality: in my limited knowledge, I do not know of other related works that investigate distribution-constrained weights of a network from a theoretical / statistical mechanics point of view

quality: the proof itself (section 2.2) is beyond me, but at a superficial level, the main result (Fig 2-4) seem solid and that simulations support the theoretical prediction. I don't really get how the teacher-student learning paradigm is relevant, however. It seems to be a bit of a contrived setting: even though it makes sense from a decoding perspective (as a neuroscientist trying to infer the weights of a neuron given only its input and outputs), no biological neuron would be required to do this as the student.

clarity: the work is clearly presented, with high quality and thoughtful visualizations, as well as an easy-to-follow high level narrative. 

significance: the high level motivation of distribution-matched learning is clearly stated, and of significant interest to the neuroscience community at large. However, the amount of assumptions / simplifications required to arrive at the actual results dampens my enthusiasm (expanded below)

Questions:
As stated above, the question of how task optimization and weight distribution matching can be simultaneously achieved, given experimental observations of log-normally distributed weights, is a very important question. However, the paper starts with this motivation, but essentially solves the problem for a single perceptron (or, uni-dimensional output logistic regression) with random inputs. I understand that assumptions are necessary for such theoretical work, which I believe is of high quality. But the massive reduction in scope feels almost like a bait and switch (though I'm sure the authors do not intend for this to be the case). At the very least, some investigation on a system that relaxes one or several of the assumptions should be done, even if the theory does not end up so clean:

- would the theory / simulation results hold for non-random inputs? Basically in no scenario does the real brain have to perform classification on perceptually random inputs, so at the minimum, a toy experiment on structured inputs would be justified (MNIST 0 vs 1, for example).

- similarly, the paper is motivated by neural "networks", so how do the results hold up for more than a single neuron? For higher dimensional outputs? Do compensation of weights for different neurons violate the theory?

- I don't see how the results of Figure 5 is relevant to the overall story. Perhaps more justification or clarity in writing needs to be provided

- in the learning algorithm, why does distribution matching have to occur separately from weight optimization? I suppose it fits nicely with the optimal transport theory, but simply optimizing a loss function that combines task performance and, e.g., DKL or Wasserstein distance between the weight distributions, simultaneously would be naively reasonable, and no less biologically implausible (since individual weights do not know of the other weights and certainly does not actively converge to some target lognormal distribution).

Limitations:
Some discussion of future works, but very little discussion of limitations in section 6. No discussion of societal impact (but none necessary)

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

In this paper, the authors develop a comprehensive theoretical and algorithmic framework for understanding perceptron learning under constraints on synaptic weight distributions. Their specific contributions include:

- A statistical mechanical theory for memory capacity of perceptrons with distribution-constrained weights, showing that capacity loss relative to unconstrained perceptrons can be expressed as the Wasserstein-2 distance between the target distribution and a standard normal distribution
- A novel learning algorithm (DisCo-SGD) that simultaneously optimizes for task performance and distribution matching through the lens of optimal transport theory and information geometry
- Application of the theory to biologically realistic scenarios with balanced excitatory and inhibitory (E/I) populations following lognormal distributions, with predictions compared against connectomic data from mouse primary auditory cortex
- An investigation of teacher-student perceptron learning where a student neuron learns to match a teacher's input-output behavior while constrained to follow a particular weight distribution, revealing that optimal performance is achieved when the student adopts distribution-dependent priors that evolve during learning

## Strengths

**Originality and Novelty**: The authors tackle a genuinely novel question at the intersection of computational neuroscience and statistical mechanics. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work to systematically investigate how prescribed weight distributions affect neural network learning capacity from a rigorous theoretical perspective. The connection between distribution constraints and Wasserstein distance (Equations 3-5) is particularly elegant and non-obvious. The insight that capacity reduction is proportional to the squared Wasserstein distance between the constrained and standard normal distributions provides a clean geometric interpretation that is both theoretically satisfying and practically useful.

**Technical Quality**: The theoretical derivation using replica symmetry and statistical mechanics appears sound, and the authors appropriately acknowledge the assumptions underlying their mean-field analysis. The support of replica symmetric solutions through numerical simulations (as discussed in Appendix A.5) strengthens confidence in the theoretical predictions. The experimental validation in Figures 2-4 demonstrates good agreement between theory and simulation across different distribution families, including the instructive example of interpolating normal distributions and the more complex case of lognormal distributions relevant to neuroscience.

**Presentation and Visualization**: The paper is exceptionally well-written with thoughtful, high-quality visualizations that effectively communicate complex concepts. Figure 2 clearly illustrates the optimal transport plan and its relationship to capacity. Figure 3 provides compelling visual evidence that training dynamics follow geodesic flows in Wasserstein space. Figure 4 effectively maps the parameter landscape and directly compares theoretical predictions with experimental measurements from connectomic data. The progression from simple examples to more complex scenarios is pedagogically sound.

**Algorithmic Contribution**: The DisCo-SGD algorithm (Table 1) represents a principled approach to finding weight configurations that simultaneously satisfy task constraints and distribution constraints. The interpretation of weight updates as geodesic flows (Section 3) provides theoretical insight into why the alternating optimization procedure works, and Figure 3 convincingly demonstrates this geometric interpretation empirically.

## Major Weaknesses

**Severe Mismatch Between Motivation and Scope**: The paper opens with compelling motivation from connectomics and neural circuits, asking how observed synaptic weight distributions constrain learning in neural networks. However, the actual theoretical and experimental analysis is restricted to single perceptrons with random, unstructured inputs—arguably the simplest possible learning scenario. While the authors acknowledge in the introduction that "previous work on brain related perceptron modeling assumes similarly unstructured data" (citing biological preprocessing), this does not adequately justify why random inputs should be the primary focus when the neuroscience motivation explicitly concerns real brain circuits operating on highly structured sensory inputs.

This scope reduction is particularly problematic because it undermines the practical relevance of the findings. Real neural circuits in sensory cortex operate on structured, highly correlated inputs with complex statistical properties. The assumption of i.i.d. Gaussian inputs with random binary labels (Section 2.1) is about as far removed from realistic neural computation as one could imagine. The authors essentially solve a toy problem that, while theoretically clean, provides limited insight into the original motivating question. This feels like a "bait and switch" where the neuroscience motivation attracts readers but the actual problem studied is far more abstract.

**Limited Architectural Scope**: The restriction to single-neuron perceptrons is another significant limitation that contradicts the motivation of understanding neural "networks." The paper is motivated by connectomic studies of neural circuits and discusses memory capacity in recurrent networks (citing work on hippocampus and cortex), yet all results concern feedforward classification by a single neuron. The extension to multiple populations in Section 4 (Equation 10) only addresses different input populations to a single output neuron, not networks of multiple neurons with distributed weight constraints.

The question of how distribution constraints on weights in different neurons interact is not addressed. For instance, if excitatory neurons in a layer must maintain lognormal weight distributions, and these weights feed into downstream inhibitory neurons that also have prescribed distributions, does the theory still hold? Do compensation mechanisms across neurons violate the assumptions? The paper provides no investigation of these questions, yet they are fundamental to understanding neural circuits.

**Unclear Relevance of Teacher-Student Framework**: While Section 5 presents technically sound analysis, the relevance of the teacher-student learning paradigm to the overall narrative is unclear and somewhat forced. The motivation given in Section 5.1 is that this setup could model circuit inference from functional recordings—a neuroscientist observing input-output relationships and attempting to infer underlying connectivity. However, this is a very specific neuroscience application that deviates substantially from the main story about how distribution constraints affect learning capacity.

More fundamentally, the biological plausibility of this scenario is questionable. Real neurons do not have access to information about the teacher's weight distribution and do not solve an optimization problem to match it. The authors argue that "there is a prior knowledge about the weight distribution of an unknown target linear classifier," but it is unclear under what biological circumstances a neuron would acquire such knowledge. The claim that distribution-constrained learning "substantially enhances the generalization performance" (Fig. 5a) is interesting but feels disconnected from the main theoretical contributions about capacity.

Furthermore, the comparison in Figure 5 between unconstrained, sign-constrained, and distribution-constrained learning is somewhat artificial. The unconstrained baseline learns a Gaussian distribution (expected from Gardner theory), sign-constrained learning leads to sparsification, and distribution-constrained learning with the correct prior performs best. But this comparison does not clearly demonstrate a general principle—it essentially shows that if you constrain weights to match the teacher's distribution, you learn better, which is somewhat tautological. The "optimal prior" results are more interesting but still rely on knowing the teacher distribution in advance.

**Algorithm Design Choices Not Well Justified**: The DisCo-SGD algorithm alternates between gradient descent on the cross-entropy loss (Equation 7) and optimal transport updates (Equation 9). While this approach is theoretically motivated by optimal transport theory, the authors do not adequately justify why this specific alternating procedure is necessary or optimal. The authors acknowledge that "performing such a one-step projection strongly interferes with the cross-entropy objective," but then propose an incremental version without rigorous justification.

A more natural approach would be to directly optimize a combined objective function that balances task performance and distribution matching, such as:
$$\mathcal{L} = \text{CrossEntropy}(w) + \lambda \cdot W_2(\hat{q}(w), q(w))^2$$

The authors dismiss this approach somewhat casually by noting it "fits nicely with the optimal transport theory," but do not explain why alternating optimization should be superior or whether the combined objective would be less "biologically implausible." In fact, one could argue that biological synapses do not have access to global information about the empirical weight distribution (required to compute the optimal transport map in Equation 8), making the algorithm itself biologically questionable. The claim that "individual weights do not know of the other weights" is valid, yet the DisCo-SGD algorithm requires computing the empirical CDF across all weights, which implicitly assumes global information.

## Minor Weaknesses and Limitations

**Restrictive Assumptions on Input Data**: The assumption of random, unstructured inputs severely limits the applicability of the results. In real sensory systems, inputs have rich statistical structure—correlations, low-dimensional manifolds, sparse representations, etc. It is not clear whether the theoretical predictions hold under more realistic input distributions. Even a simple toy experiment with structured inputs (e.g., MNIST classification with 0 vs. 1 digits as mentioned in the questions) would provide valuable evidence that the theory's insights transfer beyond the random input regime. The authors might argue that sensory preprocessing decorrelates inputs, but this is a weak justification for completely ignoring input structure.

**Replica Symmetry Assumption**: While the authors show through simulations that replica symmetry holds for the cases presented, they acknowledge in Section 2.2 that the constraint $\hat{q} = q$ is non-convex and replica symmetry "might not hold" in general. The discussion in

title: Data augmentation for efficient learning from parametric experts

They use augmentatio to improve data efficiency with learning from parametric experts. In naive data augmentation approach, parametric experts act same on augmented states and original states. However, APC get different action on augmented states using parametric experts.
They add a samll Gaussian state perturbation to state, and parametric experts predict action on perturbed state. 
They experiment on continual control tasks, and show improved data efficiency.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review

## Summary

The paper proposes Augmented Policy Cloning (APC), a data augmentation technique designed to improve the data efficiency of learning from parametric experts in reinforcement and imitation learning settings. The core insight distinguishes APC from naive data augmentation approaches: while naive augmentation applies the same action to perturbed states, APC queries the parametric expert to obtain new actions for these synthetically perturbed states. This allows the student policy to learn feedback-sensitive behavior in regions around sampled trajectories. The authors evaluate their method on several continuous control tasks from the DeepMind control suite, demonstrating improved data efficiency across multiple settings including offline behavioral cloning, expert compression, learning from privileged experts, and as a component within DAGGER and kickstarting algorithms.

## Strengths

**Well-structured presentation and clarity.** The paper is well written with clear motivation and proper experimental design. The problem formulation in Section 2 effectively contextualizes the policy cloning setting and demonstrates how it naturally arises in multiple practical scenarios—from expert compression in resource-constrained environments to transfer from privileged experts. The distinction between the proposed APC method and naive augmentation is illustrated clearly in Figure 1, making the conceptual difference immediately apparent to readers.

**Significant and consistent improvements in data efficiency.** APC demonstrates substantially improved data efficiency across all tested tasks. In the core results (Figure 2), APC achieves comparable performance to standard behavioral cloning (BC) with significantly fewer expert trajectories. For example, on the Humanoid Run task, APC achieves strong performance with 1-2 trajectories while BC requires substantially more. Notably, the naive augmented behavioral cloning (Naive ABC) baseline performs almost identically to standard BC, which effectively validates the authors' hypothesis that naive augmentation is insufficient for control problems—this negative result is informative and strengthens the paper's contribution.

**Comprehensive and well-motivated experimental evaluation.** The experimental section is exceptionally well structured, progressing logically from core offline policy cloning results to more sophisticated application scenarios. The inclusion of expert compression (Section 5.2) and learning from privileged experts (Section 5.3) demonstrates practical relevance beyond toy problems. The expert compression results are particularly compelling, showing that APC maintains data efficiency even when the student network is substantially smaller (e.g., with 65,536× fewer parameters in the [256] architecture), whereas BC performance degrades much more severely. The learning from privileged experts scenario (Figure 4) effectively demonstrates APC's utility in realistic transfer settings where the expert and student have different observation spaces.

**Broad applicability as a general technique.** The method is demonstrated as a subroutine within multiple existing algorithms (DAGGER in Section 6.1, kickstarting in Section 6.2), showing its generality. The authors provide a clear algorithmic description (Algorithm 1) that would enable practitioners to integrate APC into their own systems. The straightforward nature of the approach—simply querying the expert on perturbed states—makes it immediately applicable to any setting with a queryable parametric expert.

## Weaknesses

**Limited baseline comparisons.** The paper relies primarily on behavioral cloning and naive augmented behavioral cloning as baselines. While these are reasonable baselines for the offline policy cloning setting, the evaluation would be substantially strengthened by comparisons with other data-efficient learning approaches. The authors acknowledge in Section 7 that "one could argue that other ways to clone the expert would be to query it on densely covered state space," but do not empirically compare against such alternatives. Additionally, comparisons with other offline RL algorithms or data augmentation techniques from the broader offline RL literature (beyond the image augmentation variants tested) would provide valuable context. For instance, how does APC compare to other methods that exploit expert queryability, or to recent offline RL approaches that might be adapted to this setting? The lack of such comparisons makes it difficult to fully assess the magnitude of APC's contribution relative to the state of the art.

**Expert performance not consistently reported in figures.** While the authors include a pink dashed line representing expert performance in most figures (e.g., Figures 2-4, 6), this is not uniformly present across all results. More critically, the actual numerical expert performance values are not always clearly stated in the figure captions or main text, requiring readers to infer performance levels from the plots. In Figure 5 (DAGGER results), for instance, the expert performance line appears to show that some augmentation variants fail to match expert performance on the Run task, but the implications of this are not thoroughly discussed. Providing explicit expert performance metrics in the text would improve clarity and allow for more precise interpretation of the results.

**Insufficient analysis of perturbation magnitude sensitivity.** The paper does not provide a thorough ablation study examining how performance varies with the perturbation standard deviation σ_s. This is a critical hyperparameter: if perturbations are too small, the new expert actions may be nearly identical to the original, reducing the benefit of querying the expert; if perturbations are too large, the perturbed states may fall outside the regime where the expert provides reliable guidance. The authors do not investigate this trade-off empirically. Understanding the sensitivity of APC to this hyperparameter across different tasks and state dimensionalities would strengthen the paper and provide practical guidance for practitioners. Additionally, no justification is provided for the choice of Gaussian perturbations specifically—have the authors considered other perturbation distributions (uniform noise, structured perturbations aligned with task-relevant directions, etc.)?

**Limited exploration of alternative perturbation methods.** The paper focuses exclusively on additive Gaussian noise for state perturbations. While this is a natural choice, the authors do not explore whether other perturbation strategies might be more effective. For instance, task-specific perturbations that respect the structure of the state space, or perturbations in learned latent spaces, might be more efficient. The paper would benefit from ablation studies examining at least a few alternative perturbation schemes to demonstrate that the choice of Gaussian noise is appropriate.

**Scope limitations due to dependence on parametric experts.** As the authors acknowledge in their limitations discussion, APC fundamentally requires access to a queryable parametric expert policy. This restricts the applicability of the method to scenarios where such an expert is available. In many real-world settings, experts may only provide trajectory demonstrations without exposing their policy function for querying. This significantly limits the scope of practical applications, particularly in settings involving human demonstrations or learned policies that are not easily queryable. The authors correctly note this limitation, but it does merit more discussion about when this assumption is and is not reasonable.

**Inconsistent improvements in online learning settings.** While APC shows consistent and substantial improvements in the offline policy cloning setting (Section 5), the benefits are less pronounced in online settings. In the DAGGER experiments (Section 6.1), APC provides improvements but the gains are less dramatic than in offline settings. More problematically, in the kickstarting experiments (Section 6.2), the advantages of APC are inconsistent across tasks—improvements are visible on Humanoid Run but not on Humanoid Walls, and the authors speculate about multiple possible explanations (high-data regime, reward signal importance, Q-function learning bottleneck) without definitively identifying the cause. This suggests that APC's benefits may be limited to specific problem regimes, which should be characterized more clearly.

## Questions and Suggestions for Authors

1. **Expert quality dependency:** How sensitive is APC's performance to the quality of the expert policy? The paper trains experts to convergence, but what happens when the expert is suboptimal? This is particularly relevant for the kickstarting setting where the expert is intentionally suboptimal—do the improvements hold in such cases?

2. **Perturbation magnitude analysis:** Could the authors provide ablation studies showing APC performance as a function of perturbation standard deviation σ_s across different tasks? This would clarify the robustness of the method to this critical hyperparameter and provide practical guidance for practitioners.

3. **Alternative perturbation strategies:** Have the authors considered or tested perturbation methods beyond Gaussian noise? For instance, how would the method perform with uniform noise, or with structured perturbations designed to respect task structure?

4. **Scalability to higher dimensions:** All experiments are conducted on relatively high-dimensional continuous control tasks, but how does APC scale to extremely high-dimensional state spaces (e.g., high-resolution image observations without privileged state information)? The paper briefly addresses this for the vision setting but could provide more extensive analysis.

5. **Comparison with dense expert querying:** The authors mention that querying the expert on a densely covered state space would require exponentially more queries, but they do not empirically validate this claim or compare against such a baseline, even in a limited setting.

## Limitations

The authors appropriately discuss several limitations in Section 7. However, the scope limitation merits particular emphasis: APC can only be applied when a parametric expert policy is available for querying. This fundamentally restricts the method's applicability. In many practical scenarios—particularly those involving human demonstrations or learned policies that are not easily accessible for querying—this assumption does not hold. Consequently, while the paper makes

reviews.summary: This paper proposes an approach for imitation learning from parametric experts, by augmenting the data and then querying the experts for action labels on the new states. The authors emphasize two practical settings where learning from parametric experts is useful - expert compression and learning from privileged experts. Includes evaluation on continuous control mujoco tasks (humanoid and peg insertion).
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Originality - 

-> The proposed idea is very simple and intuitive. Data augmentation is commonly used for machine learning approaches and recently also in RL for improved performance [1,2]. Given parametric experts, querying them for action labels on the new augmented states seems like it would give rise to better performance, since more information is being extracted from the expert. This is similar to how in dagger when new states are encountered, the expert is queried for labels, except here the new states don’t require a policy rollout but rather a direct perturbation of the recorded states. While the paper does include experiments on dagger with data augmentation, I’d be curious to see how regular dagger compares to the proposed approach, in terms of policy performance and overall sample efficiency.
 
-> The paper is missing a related works section that goes into depth about prior work in the area, but the structure of the paper does include some discussion on prominent imitation learning methods (dagger, kickstarting, privileged experts) but doesn’t mention specific works adequately. 
 
[1] : Reinforcement Learning with Augmented Data (Lashkin et al.)
[2] : Regularizing Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels (Kostrikov et al.)
 
Significance - 

-> The proposed method can be of interest to a large section of the community interested in imitation learning, and is quite simple to adopt and implement. Sample efficiency is critical especially for robot learning, and the proposed approach shows the ability to learn control policies in the very low data regime. The only caveat is that the method needs parametric experts, but the authors are very clear about this and point out practical areas where the proposed approach is relevant - i.e expert compression and learning from privileged experts. The latter is specifically relevant for robotics, since sim to real pipelines [3,4] can adopt this idea and become more efficient. 
 
[3] : Rapid Motor Adaptation (Kumar et al.)
[4]: Solving Rubik’s Cube with a Robot Hand (OpenAI)
 
Quality -

-> The experimental evaluation seems quite thorough (performance is reported on 150 random environment instantiations). The authors show that the proposed approach fits the expert data much better than the baselines given an offline dataset, and the results on learning from privileged experts do seem to show that the proposed approach is effective
 
-> However, on the expert compression task, the only environment where there’s a clear advantage for the proposed method is humanoid run. On peg insertion, the baseline’s high capacity model has quite poor performance so it’s difficult to judge compression ability, and on humanoid walls the relative compression of the proposed approach and the baseline appear similar. I would recommend defining a new metric for compression (i.e ratio of performance of a low capacity model to that of the high capacity model) so that performance can be more accurately judged. 
 
Clarity - 

The paper is well written and motivated, and easy to follow. It includes clear algorithm boxes and equations that are sufficient to understand the details of the method.
reviews.questions:
-> How does the proposed approach compare to regular dagger.  Specifically, how much worse is the policy performance (if at all), if the policy isn’t rolled out to collect new states, but instead the recorded states were just perturbed to get new states, and the expert was then queried for new actions ? What are the sample efficiency gains ? 
 
-> What is the performance of the expert MPO policies on the domains reported? 
 
-> For expert compression results, please plot ratio of performance of a low capacity model to that of the high capacity model for each method for easier comparison of compression ability.
reviews.limitations: The limitation are adequately discussed, primarily being requiring a parametric expert.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes an approach for imitation learning from parametric experts, by augmenting the data and then querying the experts for action labels on the new states. The authors emphasize two practical settings where learning from parametric experts is useful - expert compression and learning from privileged experts. Includes evaluation on continuous control mujoco tasks (humanoid and peg insertion).

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality - 

-> The proposed idea is very simple and intuitive. Data augmentation is commonly used for machine learning approaches and recently also in RL for improved performance [1,2]. Given parametric experts, querying them for action labels on the new augmented states seems like it would give rise to better performance, since more information is being extracted from the expert. This is similar to how in dagger when new states are encountered, the expert is queried for labels, except here the new states don’t require a policy rollout but rather a direct perturbation of the recorded states. While the paper does include experiments on dagger with data augmentation, I’d be curious to see how regular dagger compares to the proposed approach, in terms of policy performance and overall sample efficiency.
 
-> The paper is missing a related works section that goes into depth about prior work in the area, but the structure of the paper does include some discussion on prominent imitation learning methods (dagger, kickstarting, privileged experts) but doesn’t mention specific works adequately. 
 
[1] : Reinforcement Learning with Augmented Data (Lashkin et al.)
[2] : Regularizing Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels (Kostrikov et al.)
 
Significance - 

-> The proposed method can be of interest to a large section of the community interested in imitation learning, and is quite simple to adopt and implement. Sample efficiency is critical especially for robot learning, and the proposed approach shows the ability to learn control policies in the very low data regime. The only caveat is that the method needs parametric experts, but the authors are very clear about this and point out practical areas where the proposed approach is relevant - i.e expert compression and learning from privileged experts. The latter is specifically relevant for robotics, since sim to real pipelines [3,4] can adopt this idea and become more efficient. 
 
[3] : Rapid Motor Adaptation (Kumar et al.)
[4]: Solving Rubik’s Cube with a Robot Hand (OpenAI)
 
Quality -

-> The experimental evaluation seems quite thorough (performance is reported on 150 random environment instantiations). The authors show that the proposed approach fits the expert data much better than the baselines given an offline dataset, and the results on learning from privileged experts do seem to show that the proposed approach is effective
 
-> However, on the expert compression task, the only environment where there’s a clear advantage for the proposed method is humanoid run. On peg insertion, the baseline’s high capacity model has quite poor performance so it’s difficult to judge compression ability, and on humanoid walls the relative compression of the proposed approach and the baseline appear similar. I would recommend defining a new metric for compression (i.e ratio of performance of a low capacity model to that of the high capacity model) so that performance can be more accurately judged. 
 
Clarity - 

The paper is well written and motivated, and easy to follow. It includes clear algorithm boxes and equations that are sufficient to understand the details of the method.

Questions:
-> How does the proposed approach compare to regular dagger.  Specifically, how much worse is the policy performance (if at all), if the policy isn’t rolled out to collect new states, but instead the recorded states were just perturbed to get new states, and the expert was then queried for new actions ? What are the sample efficiency gains ? 
 
-> What is the performance of the expert MPO policies on the domains reported? 
 
-> For expert compression results, please plot ratio of performance of a low capacity model to that of the high capacity model for each method for easier comparison of compression ability.

Limitations:
The limitation are adequately discussed, primarily being requiring a parametric expert.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes an approach for imitation learning from parametric experts by augmenting the data with perturbed states and then querying the experts for action labels on these new states. The core contribution is Augmented Policy Cloning (APC), which leverages the availability of parametric experts to extract feedback-sensitive behavior in regions around sampled trajectories. The authors emphasize two particularly practical settings where learning from parametric experts is useful—expert compression and learning from privileged experts—and evaluate their approach on continuous control MuJoCo tasks including humanoid and peg insertion problems.

## Strengths and Weaknesses

### Originality

The proposed idea is very simple and intuitive, which is both a strength and a limitation. Data augmentation is commonly used across machine learning approaches and has recently been shown to improve performance in RL settings [1, 2]. Given parametric experts, querying them for action labels on newly augmented states seems like it would naturally give rise to better performance, since more information is being extracted from the expert without requiring additional environment rollouts. This is conceptually similar to how DAGGER queries the expert when new states are encountered during policy rollout, except here the new states are generated through direct perturbation of recorded states rather than through actual policy rollout. The key insight distinguishing APC from naive data augmentation is well-articulated: the method maintains feedback-sensitivity by querying the expert for new actions at perturbed states, rather than assuming action invariance to state perturbations. This distinction is critical for control problems and is clearly illustrated in Figure 1.

While the paper does include experiments on DAGGER with data augmentation (Section 6.1), a more comprehensive comparison between regular DAGGER and the proposed approach would strengthen the evaluation. Specifically, it would be valuable to see a direct comparison of policy performance and overall sample efficiency between DAGGER operating with a fixed budget of expert queries (distributed over actual rollouts) versus APC with the same budget (distributed over state perturbations). This would help clarify the trade-offs between collecting data from the actual environment versus using offline perturbations.

The paper acknowledges related work on recovery from perturbations in control [Bojarski et al., 2016; George et al., 2018; Buhet et al., 2019; Laskey et al., 2017; Merel et al., 2019], but a more comprehensive related works section discussing these connections in depth would strengthen the positioning. The authors correctly note that their approach differs from prior work in not requiring domain knowledge about the perturbation model, which is a meaningful contribution. However, the paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of how the feedback-sensitivity perspective relates to classical control theory and optimal control approaches that explicitly model local feedback properties.

[1]: Reinforcement Learning with Augmented Data (Laskin et al., 2020)
[2]: Regularizing Deep Reinforcement Learning from Pixels (Kostrikov et al., 2021)

### Significance

The proposed method is of clear interest to a large section of the community interested in imitation learning and policy distillation, and it is straightforward to adopt and implement. Sample efficiency is critical especially for robot learning, and the proposed approach demonstrates the ability to learn control policies in the very low data regime—a practically important setting. The authors are appropriately transparent about the key limitation that the method requires parametric experts, and they clearly identify practical areas where this assumption is reasonable: expert compression, learning from privileged experts, DAGGER, and kickstarting.

The relevance to robotics is particularly noteworthy. Learning from privileged experts is especially pertinent for sim-to-real transfer pipelines [3, 4], where simulators provide rich state information but real robots operate with limited observations. The ability to efficiently transfer behavior from a privileged expert (with full state access) to a student policy (with only sensory observations) could meaningfully reduce the data requirements for real-world deployment. Similarly, expert compression addresses a genuine practical constraint in robotics: deploying large neural network policies on resource-constrained robotic systems. The paper demonstrates that APC can achieve comparable performance to full expert policies with significantly fewer parameters, which has direct implications for on-robot execution.

The applicability to multiple existing algorithms (behavioral cloning, DAGGER, kickstarting, and policy distillation) demonstrates the generality of the approach as a subroutine. This modularity increases the potential impact, as practitioners can integrate APC into their existing pipelines with minimal modifications.

[3]: Rapid Motor Adaptation (Kumar et al., 2020)
[4]: Solving Rubik's Cube with a Robot Hand (OpenAI, 2019)

### Quality

The experimental evaluation is thorough in several respects. Performance is reported across 150 random environment instantiations, providing good statistical coverage. The authors evaluate on three distinct high-degree-of-freedom tasks (Humanoid Run, Humanoid Walls, and Insert Peg) that present different challenges—body stabilization, visual navigation, and precise manipulation with weak reward signals respectively. The use of the DeepMind Control Suite ensures reproducibility and allows comparison with other work using these benchmarks.

The results on offline policy cloning (Figure 2) clearly demonstrate APC's advantage over baselines, with the method requiring substantially fewer expert trajectories to achieve high performance. The learning from privileged experts results (Figure 4) are particularly compelling, showing that APC enables successful transfer where standard behavioral cloning fails entirely. The Insert Peg task with vision-based observations shows that APC achieves performance comparable to the privileged information setting, whereas BC and Naive ABC fail to learn, which is a strong validation of the approach.

However, the expert compression results warrant more careful analysis. On the Humanoid Run task, there is a clear advantage for APC, particularly with larger compression ratios (e.g., when the student torso is [256] versus the baseline [256, 256, 256]). However, on the other tasks, the picture is less clear. On peg insertion, the baseline BC with the high-capacity model shows surprisingly poor performance even with 10 trajectories, which makes it difficult to assess the true compression capability. On Humanoid Walls, the relative performance improvement of APC compared to BC appears more modest across different compression ratios. To enable more rigorous comparison of compression ability, the authors should define and report a normalized compression metric—for instance, the ratio of low-capacity model performance to high-capacity model performance for each method and compression level. This would allow readers to more easily judge whether APC's improvements are consistent across compression ratios or concentrated in particular regimes.

The ablation studies in the appendices are helpful, though more analysis in the main paper would strengthen the core contributions. For instance, the sensitivity analysis with respect to the perturbation noise σ_s would be valuable to include in the main paper, as this is a key hyperparameter that practitioners need to set.

### Clarity

The paper is well written and clearly motivated. The problem formulation in Section 2 provides a useful unifying framework for understanding behavioral cloning, DAGGER, kickstarting, and other expert-driven learning approaches. The distinction between the general expert-driven learning objective (Eq. 2) and specific instantiations is pedagogically effective. Algorithm 1 clearly specifies the APC procedure, and the mathematical notation is consistent and precise throughout.

Figure 1 effectively illustrates the key distinction between APC and naive data augmentation, making the conceptual contribution immediately clear. The schematic representation of resampling actions for perturbed states versus using original actions helps readers quickly grasp why feedback-sensitivity matters for control problems.

One minor area for improvement: the paper could benefit from a more explicit discussion of the computational trade-offs. While the authors mention that APC requires M additional expert queries per state during learning (where M is the number of augmented samples per original state), a concrete analysis of wall-clock time and computational cost compared to baselines would be useful. Algorithm 1 shows that each gradient update requires querying the expert M times per batch, which could be a significant overhead depending on the expert's computational cost. This should be discussed more explicitly in the main paper.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Comparison to DAGGER**: How does the proposed approach compare to regular DAGGER in terms of policy performance and sample efficiency? Specifically, if DAGGER were constrained to use the same total number of expert queries as APC (but distributed over actual environment rollouts), how would the resulting policies compare? This comparison would help clarify whether the advantage of APC comes primarily from the data augmentation itself or from the specific way it allocates expert queries.

2. **Expert Performance Baseline**: What is the performance of the expert MPO policies on the reported domains? This baseline is important for contextualizing the results, particularly for expert compression where understanding the performance ceiling is critical for evaluating compression trade-offs.

3. **Compression Metrics**: For the expert compression results, please report a normalized compression metric such as the ratio of low-capacity model performance to high-capacity model performance for each method and compression level. This would enable clearer comparison of compression ability across different architectures and tasks.

4. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity**: How

reviews.summary: The paper introduces a data augmentation technique for behavioral cloning algorithms. It consists of adding Gaussian noise to the states used for training and storing the perturbed states and the action the expert policy would produce in such states. Using this augmented dataset, the proposed algorithm achieves better performance than the baselines over three complex continuous tasks.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well written and contains many implementation details and ablation studies.

The idea proposed is very simple and effective. Unfortunately, apart from the experimental results in complex environments, the novelty in this work is limited. It is well known that augmenting the dataset in Behavioral Cloning is useful and there are prior works applying observation perturbation and label corrections via experts queries [1,2,3].

The main motivation for using the proposed approach is the case when collecting more data in the environment is expensive, but querying the expert many times is not. The authors motivate this by saying that "an expert policy may be too large to execute due to memory considerations" and that "Many expert-driven learning approaches actually have access to an expert that can be queried", but no specific example is provided to the reader. 

The comparison with baselines BC and ABC does not seem very fair. If the policy has enough capacity and the distribution from which we sample the states covers the state-space enough, one could clone the expert by repeatedly querying it on such random states without even seeing a single trajectory. Therefore it is quite straightforward that the proposed method works better than the baselines.  

[1] M. Bojarski et al., “End to end learning for self-driving cars,” arXiv preprint, 2016

[2] L. George, T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, G. Le-Gall, and X. Perrotton, “Imitation learning for end to end vehicle longitudinal control with forward camera,” in NeurIPS, 2018.

[3] T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, and X. Perrotton, “Conditional Vehicle Trajectories Prediction in CARLA Urban Environment,” in ICCV, 2019
reviews.questions:
While in continuous control environments adding Gaussian noise to a state often produces a perturbed state that could be reached by an agent (i.e. valid states), it is not the case when operating with visual observations. Is there an intuition why data augmentation would be useful in the visual domain?

Assume that, when the expert was trained, it observes in the environment states with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ (this could be estimated from the last few trajectories during the training of the expert). What is the performance of APC when using zero trajectories and only synthetic data coming from a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$? 


Figure 1 and Algorithm 1 appear many pages before the first time they are mentioned in the paper. I suggest putting them when they are mentioned.
reviews.limitations:
The authors discuss the possible limitations of this work. The method queries the expert many times, so if expert queries are expensive, their approach might suffer. I suggest providing specific examples for this setting.

The paper introduces a data augmentation technique for behavioral cloning algorithms. It consists of adding Gaussian noise to the states used for training and storing the perturbed states and the action the expert policy would produce in such states. Using this augmented dataset, the proposed algorithm achieves better performance than the baselines over three complex continuous tasks.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well written and contains many implementation details and ablation studies.

The idea proposed is very simple and effective. Unfortunately, apart from the experimental results in complex environments, the novelty in this work is limited. It is well known that augmenting the dataset in Behavioral Cloning is useful and there are prior works applying observation perturbation and label corrections via experts queries [1,2,3].

The main motivation for using the proposed approach is the case when collecting more data in the environment is expensive, but querying the expert many times is not. The authors motivate this by saying that "an expert policy may be too large to execute due to memory considerations" and that "Many expert-driven learning approaches actually have access to an expert that can be queried", but no specific example is provided to the reader. 

The comparison with baselines BC and ABC does not seem very fair. If the policy has enough capacity and the distribution from which we sample the states covers the state-space enough, one could clone the expert by repeatedly querying it on such random states without even seeing a single trajectory. Therefore it is quite straightforward that the proposed method works better than the baselines.  

[1] M. Bojarski et al., “End to end learning for self-driving cars,” arXiv preprint, 2016

[2] L. George, T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, G. Le-Gall, and X. Perrotton, “Imitation learning for end to end vehicle longitudinal control with forward camera,” in NeurIPS, 2018.

[3] T. Buhet, E. Wirbel, and X. Perrotton, “Conditional Vehicle Trajectories Prediction in CARLA Urban Environment,” in ICCV, 2019

Questions:
While in continuous control environments adding Gaussian noise to a state often produces a perturbed state that could be reached by an agent (i.e. valid states), it is not the case when operating with visual observations. Is there an intuition why data augmentation would be useful in the visual domain?

Assume that, when the expert was trained, it observes in the environment states with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ (this could be estimated from the last few trajectories during the training of the expert). What is the performance of APC when using zero trajectories and only synthetic data coming from a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$? 


Figure 1 and Algorithm 1 appear many pages before the first time they are mentioned in the paper. I suggest putting them when they are mentioned.

Limitations:
The authors discuss the possible limitations of this work. The method queries the expert many times, so if expert queries are expensive, their approach might suffer. I suggest providing specific examples for this setting.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The paper introduces a data augmentation technique for behavioral cloning and related policy learning algorithms, termed Augmented Policy Cloning (APC). The core contribution consists of augmenting training datasets by adding Gaussian noise to states sampled from expert trajectories and, critically, querying the expert policy to obtain the appropriate action for each perturbed state. Rather than using the original action for perturbed states (as in naive data augmentation), the method leverages expert queries to create feedback-sensitive training pairs. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach across three complex continuous control tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite (Humanoid Run, Humanoid Walls, and Insert Peg), showing substantial improvements in data efficiency compared to standard behavioral cloning and naive augmentation baselines. The method is further evaluated in several practically motivated settings, including expert compression, learning from privileged experts, and as a component within DAGGER and kickstarting algorithms.

## Strengths

**1. Clear presentation and comprehensive experimental evaluation.** The paper is well-written with clear exposition of the problem setting, methodology, and experimental protocols. The authors provide extensive implementation details in the main paper and appendices, including hyperparameter specifications, network architectures, and training procedures. This level of detail significantly aids reproducibility and understanding. The paper includes ablation studies examining the impact of various design choices, and the experimental evaluation spans multiple environments and learning paradigms, demonstrating the generality of the approach.

**2. Simplicity and practical effectiveness of the core idea.** The proposed method is elegantly simple—applying Gaussian perturbations to states and querying the expert for appropriate actions—yet demonstrates consistent and often substantial empirical improvements across diverse settings. This simplicity is a significant strength, as it makes the approach easy to understand, implement, and integrate into existing algorithms. The straightforward nature of the method also suggests it could be readily adopted in practice.

**3. Well-motivated problem formulation.** The paper effectively motivates the "policy cloning" setting by discussing multiple practical scenarios where expert policies are available for querying: expert compression for deployment on resource-constrained devices (as in Parisotto and Salakhutdinov [2021]), distillation of policies with privileged information into student policies without such information, integration with algorithms like DAGGER that naturally support expert queries, and kickstarting suboptimal experts. These motivations help establish the relevance of the problem beyond theoretical interest.

**4. Comprehensive evaluation across multiple learning paradigms.** Beyond the core offline policy cloning experiments, the authors demonstrate APC's utility as a component within online learning algorithms (DAGGER) and RL algorithms with expert guidance (kickstarting). This breadth of evaluation strengthens the claim that the approach is generally applicable. The inclusion of expert compression and privileged expert transfer experiments addresses practically important scenarios that distinguish this work from purely theoretical contributions.

**5. Thoughtful comparison with related work.** The authors acknowledge and discuss related prior work on data augmentation with expert feedback, including approaches in autonomous driving (Bojarski et al., 2016; George et al., 2018; Buhet et al., 2019). They position their contribution as requiring no domain knowledge while still leveraging the key insight about feedback-sensitive augmentation, which is a valuable clarification of novelty.

## Weaknesses and Concerns

**1. Limited novelty given existing work on expert-aware augmentation.** While the paper makes useful empirical contributions, the core conceptual novelty is limited. The fundamental insight that augmenting training data with expert-corrected labels for perturbed inputs improves learning has been explored in prior work, as the authors themselves acknowledge through citations to Bojarski et al. (2016), George et al. (2018), and Buhet et al. (2019). These prior works demonstrated similar principles in autonomous driving contexts, using observation perturbations with expert-provided label corrections. Although the current work applies this principle more broadly and without requiring domain-specific knowledge of the perturbation model, the core idea of "perturb state, query expert for corrected action" is not fundamentally novel. The paper would be strengthened by a more thorough empirical comparison with these prior approaches adapted to the current domains, rather than primarily comparing against BC and naive ABC.

**2. Questionable fairness and design of baseline comparisons.** The comparison with baselines raises important questions about experimental design. The paper primarily compares against standard behavioral cloning (BC) and naive augmented behavioral cloning (Naive ABC), where Naive ABC uses the original action for all perturbed states. However, as the reviewer notes, if one has sufficient expert query budget, the most straightforward approach would be to simply query the expert on a densely-sampled set of random states without requiring any environment trajectories at all. This raises a fundamental question: the comparison is somewhat circular in that APC is compared against methods that are not designed to use expert queries effectively. A more compelling comparison would include a baseline that systematically covers the state space through random state sampling and expert queries, which would more directly establish the advantage of APC's trajectory-focused perturbation strategy. Without such a comparison, it is unclear whether the benefits of APC derive from the specific choice to perturb around trajectories or simply from more effective use of expert queries compared to the baselines.

**3. Unclear motivation for the specific problem setting.** While the paper provides several scenarios where expert policies are available, the fundamental assumption—that expert queries are cheap but environment interactions are expensive—requires more concrete justification. The motivating examples provided (expert compression, privileged information distillation, DAGGER, kickstarting) are reasonable but somewhat abstract. The paper would be significantly strengthened by providing specific, concrete examples from real robotics or other domains where this cost structure actually holds in practice. For instance, in robotics, what does it mean to "query" an expert policy? Does it require running the expert on the physical robot? If so, why would this be cheaper than running the student policy? The paper discusses this briefly in Section 7 but does not provide sufficient concrete grounding for the practical importance of the assumption.

**4. Problematic application to visual observations.** A critical unresolved question concerns the application of APC to visual observations. The paper includes experiments on the Humanoid Walls task, which involves egocentric image observations, and combines state-based APC with image augmentation. However, the paper does not adequately address a fundamental issue: when applying Gaussian noise to pixel observations, the resulting perturbed images may not correspond to valid states that could actually be reached through environment dynamics. Unlike continuous state spaces (e.g., joint angles, positions) where small Gaussian perturbations typically produce valid states, arbitrary pixel-level perturbations often produce unrealistic observations. The paper mentions using "random crops" for image augmentation but does not discuss whether these augmented images represent plausible observations. This is a significant limitation that affects the theoretical justification for why APC should work in the visual domain. The paper would benefit from explicit discussion of this issue and either theoretical or empirical analysis of whether the augmented visual states are meaningful, or acknowledgment that the approach may be fundamentally limited to state-based observations.

**5. Missing analysis of the synthetic data regime.** One of the reviewer's questions—what would be the performance if APC used only synthetic data from a distribution matching the expert's observed state distribution, without any real trajectories—is not addressed. This is a revealing question because it would directly test whether APC's benefits come from the specific trajectory-focused perturbation strategy or simply from having more expert queries. If performance with zero trajectories and only synthetic states is comparable to APC with few trajectories, this would suggest that the trajectory-focused aspect is not critical. Conversely, if performance degrades substantially, this would more convincingly demonstrate the value of the trajectory-focused approach. The authors should provide this analysis to strengthen claims about the specific contribution of their method.

**6. Inconsistent results in the kickstarting setting.** The kickstarting experiments (Section 6.2) show less consistent improvements compared to the offline policy cloning and DAGGER experiments. The authors provide several reasonable hypotheses for this (high-data regime reducing relative importance of augmentation, need to learn Q-functions that cannot benefit from APC, lack of annealing schedule for the cloning weight), but these explanations suggest the method may have limited applicability in settings where RL objectives dominate. This limitation deserves more prominent discussion and could indicate that APC is most valuable in the pure imitation learning regime rather than as a general technique for expert-guided RL.

**7. Limited scope of experimental domains.** While the three chosen tasks (Humanoid Run, Humanoid Walls, Insert Peg) are complex and high-dimensional, they represent a relatively narrow slice of continuous control problems. All experiments use the same physics simulator (MuJoCo) and the same environment suite (dm_control). The generality of the approach would be more convincingly demonstrated through evaluation on additional domains with different characteristics, such as locomotion tasks with different morphologies, manipulation tasks with different dynamics properties, or tasks from other simulators. This would strengthen claims about the broad applicability of the method.

**8. Incomplete discussion of computational costs.** While the paper mentions that APC requires M additional expert

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a simple yet effective way APC, which queries the parametric experts to augment the demonstrations for imitation learning. APC is shown to outperform naive behavior cloning in both expert compression and transferring from privileged expert settings.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Pros:
This paper focuses on a novel problem where a parametric expert can be queried. The paper writing is clear and easy to follow. The empirical results do demonstrate the advantage of APC over BC and naive ABC. 

Cons:
However, I don't feel this paper proposes any novel idea. One serious issue is that when compared with baselines, all these baselines don't query the expert as APC does. This means that the baselines have fewer demonstrations to imitate from. It is clear that APC will beat naive ABC since APC has more high-quality/ground-truth demonstrations. Therefore, I think this doesn't make enough technical contributions to the problem it studies.
reviews.questions:
I think the key problem here is that the authors don't explore the methods of querying experts enough. From my point of view, the advantage of APC is totally brought by a new setting where the expert could be queried, which is not an innovation of the methods. I suggest the authors compare APC with more baselines that can query the experts. 

Although this method is reasonable, I'm afraid the current technical contribution is far from enough. Adding the Gaussian noise is the simplest way to make a data augmentation, and previous works (e.g., RAD, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2004.14990.pdf) also have systematically studied the data augmentations for general reinforcement learning problems. It could be better if the authors come up with novel methods for data augmentations in your settings.
reviews.limitations: The authors have addressed the limitations.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Pros:
This paper focuses on a novel problem where a parametric expert can be queried. The paper writing is clear and easy to follow. The empirical results do demonstrate the advantage of APC over BC and naive ABC. 

Cons:
However, I don't feel this paper proposes any novel idea. One serious issue is that when compared with baselines, all these baselines don't query the expert as APC does. This means that the baselines have fewer demonstrations to imitate from. It is clear that APC will beat naive ABC since APC has more high-quality/ground-truth demonstrations. Therefore, I think this doesn't make enough technical contributions to the problem it studies.

Questions:
I think the key problem here is that the authors don't explore the methods of querying experts enough. From my point of view, the advantage of APC is totally brought by a new setting where the expert could be queried, which is not an innovation of the methods. I suggest the authors compare APC with more baselines that can query the experts. 

Although this method is reasonable, I'm afraid the current technical contribution is far from enough. Adding the Gaussian noise is the simplest way to make a data augmentation, and previous works (e.g., RAD, https://arxiv.org/pdf/2004.14990.pdf) also have systematically studied the data augmentations for general reinforcement learning problems.
